enrichment.short_description: ContactOut is a Chrome extension and web platform that finds verified personal emails and phone numbers from LinkedIn profiles for recruiters and sales teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ContactOut finds verified emails and phone numbers from LinkedIn profiles. See 2026 pricing, key features, pros and cons, and how it compares to rivals.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ContactOut is a contact-data and email-finding platform founded in 2015 by Rob Liu and Morny Zhang, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company employs roughly 90 people and serves more than 1.4 million recruiters, sales representatives, and marketers, including staff at Fortune 500 companies. Its core product is a Chrome browser extension plus a web dashboard and API that surface personal (non-work) email addresses and mobile phone numbers for professionals found on LinkedIn, GitHub, and other public profiles, claiming coverage for roughly 75% of the people users search for, drawn from a database described as covering more than 700 million profiles. ContactOut is positioned against competitors such as Apollo.io, Lusha, Hunter.io, RocketReach, and SignalHire in the sales-intelligence and recruiting-tech category. The product line splits into two main use cases: sales prospecting (finding buyer contact details to fuel outbound outreach and CRM enrichment) and recruiting (sourcing passive candidates and enriching applicant records for applicant tracking systems). ContactOut integrates with Salesforce and major ATS platforms, and offers bulk search, list building, and data export tools alongside its per-profile lookup extension. Pricing follows a freemium model: a free tier provides a small daily allotment of email, phone, and personal-email lookups (reported as five of each per day); paid plans include an Email plan for sales users (around $49 per month billed annually, or roughly $79 billed monthly, capped at about 2,000 email credits per month), a combined Email + Phone plan (around $99 per month annually, with roughly 2,000 email and 1,000 phone credits), and a Recruiter plan (around $199 per month annually) aimed at talent teams that need ATS integrations and higher credit caps. Team and enterprise pricing, including an API/bulk-license option and a reported unlimited-sales-user tier near $299 per month, is quoted directly by ContactOut's sales team, and multiple independent reviews note that "unlimited" marketing on higher tiers is subject to fair-use credit caps not always shown on the public pricing page. ContactOut does not publish its underlying data-sourcing methodology in detail, and third-party reviewers highlight variable data accuracy for phone numbers versus emails as a common trade-off with this category of contact-enrichment tool. The product is delivered as a SaaS web application and a Chrome extension; there is no self-hosted or open-source version. For teams evaluating ContactOut, the main decision points are: daily and monthly credit caps versus actual outreach volume needed, whether Salesforce or ATS integration is required, and how ContactOut's per-credit economics compare to competitors like Apollo or Lusha for the specific mix of email versus direct-dial phone lookups a team needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ContactOut is a contact-discovery platform, best known for its Chrome extension, that reveals verified personal emails and phone numbers for professionals found on LinkedIn and other public profiles. Founded in 2015 by Rob Liu and Morny Zhang and headquartered in San Francisco, the company serves recruiters, sales teams, and marketers who need to reach people directly rather than through generic work inboxes.

The platform is used both for sales prospecting and for talent sourcing, and integrates with Salesforce and applicant tracking systems so contact data can flow directly into existing sales and recruiting workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Chrome extension surfaces contact details in one click while browsing LinkedIn or other profile pages, drawing on a database described as covering more than 700 million professional profiles. Bulk search and list-building tools let teams enrich many contacts at once instead of looking people up one at a time.
Recruiter-focused plans add ATS integrations and higher lookup caps, while a Team/API plan supports Salesforce syncing, team usage reporting, and a dedicated account manager for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ContactOut uses a freemium model. A free plan gives a small daily allotment of email and phone lookups with no credit card required. Paid plans scale from an Email plan around $49/month (billed annually) for sales use, to a combined Email + Phone plan around $99/month, to a Recruiter plan around $199/month with ATS integrations.
Team, API, and unlimited-user tiers are quoted directly by ContactOut's sales team; several independent reviews note that credit caps still apply on plans marketed as unlimited.

features.title: LinkedIn Chrome Extension
features.description: One-click reveal of verified personal emails and phone numbers directly on LinkedIn and other profile pages.

features.title: Large Contact Database
features.description: Search across a database described as covering more than 700 million professional profiles.

features.title: Bulk Search & Lists
features.description: Upload lists or build saved searches to enrich many contacts at once instead of one-by-one lookups.

features.title: ATS & CRM Integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Salesforce and applicant tracking systems for recruiting teams.

features.title: API Access
features.description: Programmatic access to contact lookups for teams building custom workflows on the Team/API plan.

features.title: Team Management & Reporting
features.description: Usage dashboards, seat management, and a dedicated account manager on higher tiers.

features.title: Free Daily Credits
features.description: A free tier grants a small daily allotment of email and phone lookups with no credit card required.

pros: Fast, one-click lookups directly inside LinkedIn via the Chrome extension
Separate plans for sales versus recruiting use cases, including ATS integrations
Free tier lets teams test data accuracy before buying
Large claimed database with both email and phone coverage
cons: Monthly credit caps apply even on plans marketed as unlimited
Phone number accuracy is reported as less reliable than email accuracy
Data-sourcing methodology is not published in detail
Annual billing is required to access the lowest advertised per-month prices

faqs.question: What is ContactOut used for?
faqs.answer: ContactOut is used to find verified personal emails and phone numbers for people found on LinkedIn and other professional profiles, mainly for sales outreach and recruiting.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of ContactOut?
faqs.answer: Yes. ContactOut offers a free tier with a small daily allotment of email, phone, and personal-email lookups and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Does ContactOut integrate with Salesforce or an ATS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Salesforce and applicant tracking system integrations are available, primarily on the Recruiter and Team/API plans.

faqs.question: How much does ContactOut cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start around $49/month for sales-focused email lookups and scale up to around $199/month for recruiter plans with ATS integrations; team and enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Who founded ContactOut?
faqs.answer: ContactOut was founded in 2015 by Rob Liu and Morny Zhang in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does ContactOut work outside of LinkedIn?
faqs.answer: The Chrome extension is primarily used on LinkedIn but can also surface contact data on other profile and company pages.

use_cases.title: Outbound Sales Prospecting
use_cases.description: Sales reps find verified emails and phone numbers for target buyers to fuel cold outreach and CRM enrichment.

use_cases.title: Passive Candidate Sourcing
use_cases.description: Recruiters locate and contact passive candidates found on LinkedIn, syncing results into an ATS.

enrichment.short_description: Contao is a free, open-source PHP content management system for building accessible, secure websites, self-hosted and maintained by a community and the Contao Association.
enrichment.seo_title: Contao CMS: Features, Pricing, and Overview
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Contao is a free, open-source PHP CMS for accessible, secure websites. Learn about its history, features, self-hosting requirements, and community support.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Contao is a free, open-source content management system written in PHP, originally released in 2006 under the name TYPOLight before being renamed Contao in 2010 to avoid confusion with the similarly named TYPO3 CMS. Since version 4, released in 2015, Contao has been built on the Symfony PHP framework, and the project is licensed under the LGPL-3.0 open-source license, meaning it can be downloaded, self-hosted, and modified free of charge. Contao is maintained by a community of core developers coordinated in large part through the nonprofit Contao Association, which anyone can join as a paying member to help fund ongoing development, and by contributing agencies such as terminal42 gmbh, based in Lyss, Switzerland, one of the most active long-term contributors to the Contao core and its official Contao Manager installation tool. Contao ships as a full CMS: it includes a structured back end for managing pages, articles, and content elements; a built-in newsletter module; form generation; a calendar and event-listing module; multi-language site support; and accessibility features that reflect the project's original focus on producing barrier-free websites, a priority since Contao has historically been used by German public-sector and accessibility-focused organizations. Because it runs on Symfony, Contao supports modern PHP development practices, Composer-based dependency management via Packagist, and a large extension ecosystem contributed by third-party developers and agencies. Contao requires a standard PHP and MySQL/MariaDB hosting stack and is typically self-hosted on a VPS, dedicated server, or specialized Contao hosting offered by partner agencies, rather than delivered as a managed SaaS product. There is no proprietary paid tier of Contao itself: the core software is free to download and use, and the project's revenue model instead centers on the Contao Association's membership fees, paid extensions and themes sold by third-party developers on the Contao extension marketplace, and paid implementation, hosting, and support services offered by certified Contao partner agencies such as terminal42. This makes Contao's total cost of ownership dependent on hosting choice, developer or agency fees, and any premium extensions selected, rather than a fixed software subscription. Contao competes in the open-source CMS space against WordPress, TYPO3, Drupal, and ProcessWire, and is most commonly chosen by organizations that want a lightweight but full-featured, self-hosted, Symfony-based CMS with strong out-of-the-box accessibility and multilingual support, particularly in the German-speaking European market where it originated and remains most widely adopted. Governance is community-driven rather than controlled by a single commercial vendor, with release planning, security patches, and roadmap discussion happening publicly on GitHub and at Contao core developer meetups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Contao is a free, open-source content management system written in PHP. It first appeared in 2006 as TYPOLight and was renamed Contao in 2010. Since version 4 (2015), it has been built on the Symfony framework and is licensed under LGPL-3.0, so it can be downloaded, self-hosted, and modified at no cost.

Development is coordinated by a community of core contributors, the nonprofit Contao Association, and partner agencies such as terminal42 gmbh in Lyss, Switzerland, which has maintained Contao extensions and the official Contao Manager for more than a decade.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Contao provides a full CMS toolset: page and content-element management, a newsletter module, form building, a calendar/event module, and multi-language site support. Accessibility has been a core design priority since the project's early years, and Contao is widely used by organizations that need WCAG-conformant public-facing sites.

Because it runs on Symfony, Contao supports Composer-based extension installation via Packagist and integrates with modern PHP development workflows, giving it a large third-party extension ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Contao itself is free open-source software with no license fee. Costs instead come from self-hosting (server and database infrastructure), optional Contao Association membership, premium extensions sold on the Contao extension marketplace, and implementation or support services from certified Contao partner agencies.

features.title: Content Element Management
features.description: Structured back-end tools for building pages out of reusable content elements.

features.title: Accessibility-first Output
features.description: Templates and back-end conventions oriented around producing barrier-free, WCAG-conformant websites.

features.title: Multi-language Site Support
features.description: Built-in tools for running and translating multilingual websites from one installation.

features.title: Newsletter & Form Modules
features.description: Native newsletter sending and form-building modules without third-party plugins.

features.title: Symfony-based Architecture
features.description: Built on Symfony since version 4, with Composer-based extension management via Packagist.

features.title: Self-hosted Deployment
features.description: Runs on standard PHP and MySQL/MariaDB hosting, giving full control over data and infrastructure.

pros: Free and open source, with no software licensing fees
Strong accessibility and WCAG track record
Built on Symfony, familiar to PHP developers
Active community and third-party extension marketplace
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing PHP/MySQL server management
Smaller ecosystem and developer talent pool than WordPress
Most documentation and community activity is concentrated in German-speaking markets
No official managed hosting from the core project itself

faqs.question: Is Contao free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Contao is free, open-source software licensed under LGPL-3.0; costs come from hosting, optional extensions, and any paid implementation support.

faqs.question: What was Contao previously called?
faqs.answer: Contao was originally released in 2006 as TYPOLight and renamed Contao in 2010.

faqs.question: What does Contao run on?
faqs.answer: Contao requires PHP and a MySQL or MariaDB database, and since version 4 has been built on the Symfony framework.

faqs.question: Who maintains Contao?
faqs.answer: Contao is maintained by a community of core developers, the nonprofit Contao Association, and contributing agencies such as terminal42 gmbh.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Contao?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting on a VPS, dedicated server, or partner-agency hosting is the standard deployment model.

faqs.question: Does Contao support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-language site management is a built-in feature.

use_cases.title: Accessible Public-Sector Websites
use_cases.description: Government and institutional sites use Contao's accessibility-oriented output to meet barrier-free web requirements.

use_cases.title: Multilingual Corporate Websites
use_cases.description: Agencies build multilingual business websites on Contao's Symfony-based, self-hosted architecture.
use_cases.audience: SMBs and agencies in German-speaking Europe

enrichment.short_description: Contentful is an API-first headless CMS that lets teams manage structured content once and deliver it to websites, apps, and other channels via APIs.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Contentful is an API-first headless CMS for structured content. Compare 2026 pricing plans, key features, integrations, pros and cons, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Contentful is a headless, API-first content management system founded in 2013 in Berlin, Germany by Sascha Konietzke and Paolo Negri. The company has grown into a dual-headquartered organization with major offices in Berlin and San Francisco, plus additional locations in Denver, New York, Sydney, and London, and reports roughly 1,000 employees as of 2026. Rather than coupling content storage to a specific front-end templating system the way traditional CMS platforms like WordPress do, Contentful stores content as structured, reusable data ("content models" made of custom fields and entries) and exposes it through REST and GraphQL Content Delivery and Content Management APIs, letting engineering teams render that content on websites, mobile apps, IoT devices, in-store displays, or any other channel from a single source of truth. This headless, composable architecture is Contentful's core differentiator and is aimed primarily at mid-market and enterprise engineering and content teams that need to publish the same content across multiple digital properties or need tighter control over front-end frameworks such as React, Next.js, and Vue than a monolithic CMS allows. Contentful's product has expanded beyond core content storage to include an editorial workspace (comments, task management, scheduled publishing, live multi-user collaboration), governance and workflow tools, and Ninetailed-powered personalization and experimentation features (A/B testing, audience segmentation, AI content suggestions) sold as an add-on Personalization product line, alongside newer AI Actions and AI Studio offerings for generative content workflows. Pricing is structured in tiers: a Free/Community plan supports up to 10 users, 100,000 API calls per month, and 50 GB of CDN bandwidth on a single Starter space; a paid Lite plan is priced at €300 per month and raises limits to 20 users, 1 million API calls, and 100 GB bandwidth while adding collaboration and scheduling features; and Enterprise pricing is fully custom, offering unlimited API calls, custom roles and locales, dedicated support, and SLAs up to 99.99% uptime. The Personalization product is priced separately in Start, Core, and Scale tiers based on monthly active profiles (100K, 500K, and 1M respectively) and AI-suggestion allowances. Independent buyer guides note that real-world Contentful bills often run 40-60% above the advertised headline price once a team's actual API call volume, space count, and environment usage are factored in, since usage-based overages and add-on spaces are common at scale. Contentful competes with other headless and composable CMS platforms such as Sanity, Strapi, Storyblok, and Contentstack, and with traditional CMSs like WordPress and Adobe Experience Manager for enterprise content operations. It integrates with major front-end frameworks via SDKs, and with commerce, marketing, and DAM tools through its App Framework and marketplace of pre-built connectors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Contentful is a headless, API-first content management system founded in 2013 in Berlin by Sascha Konietzke and Paolo Negri. It now operates from dual headquarters in Berlin and San Francisco with additional offices worldwide, and employs roughly 1,000 people.

Instead of coupling content to a specific website template, Contentful stores structured content and delivers it via REST and GraphQL APIs to websites, mobile apps, and other channels, making it a popular choice for enterprise and mid-market teams building composable digital experiences.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include flexible content modeling, REST and GraphQL Content Delivery and Management APIs, environments for staging content changes, and an editorial workspace with comments, task management, and scheduled publishing.

Beyond core CMS functionality, Contentful offers Ninetailed-powered personalization and A/B testing as an add-on product, plus emerging AI Actions and AI Studio tooling for generative content workflows, and an App Framework for integrating with commerce, marketing, and DAM systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Contentful's platform pricing has three tiers: a Free plan (10 users, 100K API calls/month, 50GB bandwidth), a Lite plan at €300/month (20 users, 1M API calls/month, 100GB bandwidth, plus collaboration features), and custom Enterprise pricing with unlimited API calls and dedicated support.

Personalization is priced separately in Start, Core, and Scale tiers based on monthly active profiles, and AI Actions/Studio pricing is quote-based. Buyer guides note usage-based overages commonly push real bills above the headline price.

features.title: Structured Content Modeling
features.description: Define custom content types and fields that can be reused across any channel.

features.title: REST & GraphQL APIs
features.description: Content Delivery and Content Management APIs for pulling and pushing structured content programmatically.

features.title: Multi-channel Delivery
features.description: Publish the same content to websites, mobile apps, and other digital touchpoints from one source of truth.

features.title: Editorial Collaboration Tools
features.description: Comments, task management, scheduled publishing, and live multi-user collaboration.

features.title: Personalization & Experimentation
features.description: Ninetailed-powered A/B testing and audience segmentation, sold as an add-on Personalization product.

features.title: AI Actions & AI Studio
features.description: AI-assisted content generation and workflow tooling, available on custom pricing.

features.title: App Framework & Integrations
features.description: Marketplace of pre-built apps connecting Contentful to commerce, marketing, and DAM platforms.

features.title: Environments & Versioning
features.description: Isolated environments for staging and testing content and schema changes before publishing.

pros: Highly flexible for multi-channel and composable architectures
Strong developer experience with REST/GraphQL APIs and SDKs
Scales to enterprise governance needs like custom roles and SLAs
Active ecosystem of integrations and apps
cons: Pricing can escalate quickly with API call and space overages
Requires front-end development resources; not a drag-and-drop site builder
Personalization and advanced AI features are sold as separate paid add-ons
Steeper learning curve than traditional CMS platforms for non-technical users

faqs.question: What is Contentful?
faqs.answer: Contentful is a headless, API-first content management system that stores structured content and delivers it to websites, apps, and other channels via APIs.

faqs.question: Is Contentful free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Contentful offers a free Community tier for up to 10 users with 100,000 API calls per month before paid plans are needed.

faqs.question: How much does Contentful cost?
faqs.answer: Contentful is free to start, with a Lite plan at €300/month and custom Enterprise pricing for larger organizations.

faqs.question: Who founded Contentful?
faqs.answer: Contentful was founded in 2013 in Berlin, Germany by Sascha Konietzke and Paolo Negri.

faqs.question: Does Contentful support GraphQL?
faqs.answer: Yes, alongside REST, Contentful offers a GraphQL Content Delivery API.

faqs.question: Is Contentful self-hosted?
faqs.answer: No, Contentful is a cloud-hosted SaaS platform with no self-hosted deployment option.

use_cases.title: Omnichannel Content Delivery
use_cases.description: Enterprises manage content once and publish it across web, mobile, and other channels via APIs.

use_cases.title: Composable Website & App Builds
use_cases.description: Development teams pair Contentful with frameworks like React or Next.js for custom front ends.
use_cases.audience: Digital agencies and product engineering teams

use_cases.title: Personalized Web Experiences
use_cases.description: Marketing teams run A/B tests and audience segmentation using Contentful's Personalization add-on.

enrichment.short_description: Contentsquare offers heatmaps, session replay, and AI-driven experience analytics for enterprise UX, product, and marketing teams optimizing websites and apps.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Contentsquare offers heatmaps, session replay, and AI experience analytics for enterprise UX teams. See key features, pricing model, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Contentsquare is a digital experience analytics platform founded in 2012 in Paris, France, by Jonathan Cherki, who started the company as a student project before building it into one of the largest independent experience-analytics vendors. Headquartered in Paris with offices across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific, Contentsquare employs approximately 1,800 people as of 2026 and has raised roughly $1.42 billion in total funding across multiple rounds, including a $500 million Series E in 2021 and a $600 million Series F in 2022, reaching a reported $5.6 billion valuation; investors include BlackRock, KKR, SoftBank Vision Fund 2, Highland Europe, and Eurazeo. The platform is built for product, UX, marketing, and e-commerce teams that need to understand how real users behave on websites and mobile apps beyond what standard web analytics such as pageviews and conversion funnels can show. Its core capabilities include zone-based heatmaps that visualize where users click, scroll, and hover; full session replay so teams can watch anonymized recordings of individual user journeys; journey and funnel analysis to find drop-off points; AI-powered anomaly detection that automatically flags unusual spikes in errors, rage clicks, or conversion drops; and error and frustration-signal monitoring, such as rage clicks, dead clicks, and JavaScript errors, that surfaces friction without requiring teams to manually review recordings. Contentsquare has grown through acquisitions, including session-replay and product-analytics companies, to broaden its platform beyond web analytics into a fuller experience-intelligence suite covering web, mobile app, and, through partnerships, voice-of-customer and merchandising data for retail and e-commerce brands. The product is delivered as an enterprise SaaS platform with a JavaScript/SDK-based tag deployed on customer sites and apps, plus a dashboard, an API for exporting insights into BI tools, and integrations with major tag managers, CDPs, and marketing platforms such as Google Analytics, Adobe Experience Cloud, Salesforce, and Optimizely. Contentsquare does not publish self-service or per-seat pricing publicly; enterprise pricing is quoted individually by its sales team based on traffic volume, number of sites and apps monitored, and which modules, such as session replay, mobile app analytics, or merchandising, a customer licenses, which is typical for large-scale experience analytics vendors given the data volumes involved. This makes Contentsquare best suited to mid-market and enterprise organizations with dedicated UX, conversion rate optimization, or digital product teams rather than solo site owners or small businesses, and it primarily competes with platforms such as FullStory, Hotjar, Microsoft Clarity, Quantum Metric, and Adobe Analytics and Target in the behavioral and experience analytics category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Contentsquare is a digital experience analytics platform founded in 2012 in Paris by Jonathan Cherki. The company is headquartered in Paris with offices across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific, employs roughly 1,800 people, and has raised approximately $1.42 billion in funding from investors including BlackRock, KKR, and SoftBank Vision Fund 2.
The platform helps product, UX, marketing, and e-commerce teams understand real user behavior on websites and mobile apps, going beyond standard pageview and conversion-funnel analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Contentsquare combines zone-based heatmaps, full session replay, and journey/funnel analysis with AI-powered anomaly detection that automatically flags unusual spikes in errors or conversion drops.

Error and frustration-signal monitoring, such as rage clicks and dead clicks, surfaces friction automatically, and the platform extends to mobile app analytics and merchandising insights for e-commerce and retail brands through acquisitions and partnerships.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Contentsquare does not publish self-service pricing. Enterprise pricing is quoted by its sales team based on traffic volume, the number of sites and apps monitored, and which modules, such as session replay or mobile analytics, a customer licenses.

features.title: Mobile App Analytics
features.description: Extends heatmap and session-level insight to native mobile app experiences.

features.title: Enterprise Integrations
features.description: Connects with Google Analytics, Adobe Experience Cloud, Salesforce, and Optimizely.

pros: Deep behavioral insight beyond standard web analytics
AI-driven anomaly and frustration detection reduces manual review
Broad enterprise integration ecosystem
Covers both web and mobile app experiences
cons: No public or self-serve pricing; requires a sales conversation
Enterprise-oriented cost and complexity may not suit small teams
Implementation requires tagging and SDK setup with technical resourcing
Full feature set spans multiple licensed modules, adding cost complexity

faqs.question: What does Contentsquare do?
faqs.answer: Contentsquare provides digital experience analytics, including heatmaps, session replay, and AI-powered insight into how users behave on websites and apps.

faqs.question: How much does Contentsquare cost?
faqs.answer: Contentsquare does not publish public pricing; it quotes enterprise contracts based on traffic volume and licensed modules.

faqs.question: Who founded Contentsquare?
faqs.answer: Contentsquare was founded in 2012 in Paris, France, by Jonathan Cherki.

faqs.question: Does Contentsquare offer session replay?
faqs.answer: Yes, full anonymized session replay is a core feature of the platform.

faqs.question: Does Contentsquare have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, an API is available for exporting insights into other BI and analytics tools.

faqs.question: Is Contentsquare good for mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, Contentsquare includes dedicated mobile app analytics alongside its web product.

enrichment.short_description: ContentStudio is an all-in-one social media management platform for scheduling, AI content creation, analytics, and team collaboration across major social networks.
enrichment.headquarters: Claymont, Delaware, US (with operations in Lahore and Islamabad, Pakistan)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ContentStudio is a social media management platform with AI writing, scheduling, and analytics. Compare 2026 pricing plans, key features, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ContentStudio is a social media and content marketing management platform founded in 2017 by Waqar Azeem, with development and support teams based in Lahore and Islamabad, Pakistan, and a US corporate registration in Claymont, Delaware. The product targets marketers, social media managers, agencies, and small-to-midsize businesses that need to plan, create, schedule, publish, and analyze content across many social channels and blogs from a single dashboard, competing with tools such as Hootsuite, Buffer, Sprout Social, and Later in the social media management category. Core functionality includes a unified content calendar and scheduler covering Facebook, Instagram, X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, Pinterest, Threads, and Google Business Profile, plus blog publishing integrations for WordPress, Medium, and Shopify. ContentStudio also includes content discovery tools that surface trending articles and topics in a given niche, an AI writing assistant for drafting captions and post copy, AI image generation for visual content, a unified social inbox that consolidates comments and messages from connected accounts for engagement management, influencer discovery tools, and analytics and reporting dashboards to track post and campaign performance. For agencies and larger marketing teams, the platform adds content approval workflows so clients or managers can review and sign off on posts before they go live, team collaboration features, a media library reported at 15 GB of storage, API key management for custom integrations, and native automation integrations with Zapier, Make, n8n, and Pabbly Connect. Pricing follows a tiered subscription model without a permanent free plan: a Standard plan starts at roughly $19 per month for a single user managing up to 5 social accounts; an Advanced plan runs approximately $49 per month for a single user with up to 25 connected accounts; and an Agency Unlimited plan is priced around $99 per month, removing the user-seat limit and supporting up to 50 connected accounts, aimed at agencies managing multiple client accounts under one roof. ContentStudio offers a free trial rather than an always-free tier, which distinguishes its go-to-market approach from freemium competitors like Buffer. Because pricing tiers gate access primarily by the number of connected social accounts and users rather than by post volume, ContentStudio tends to be priced competitively for growing agencies and multi-brand marketing teams that need to manage many accounts under a single login, though solo creators focused on a single platform may find dedicated single-network tools cheaper. The platform is delivered entirely as a cloud-based web application, with no self-hosted or open-source option, and browser and mobile-web access for on-the-go publishing and monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ContentStudio is a social media and content marketing management platform founded in 2017 by Waqar Azeem, with teams based in Lahore and Islamabad, Pakistan, and US registration in Claymont, Delaware. It targets marketers, agencies, and small-to-midsize businesses managing multiple social accounts.
The platform competes with tools like Hootsuite, Buffer, and Sprout Social, differentiating with built-in AI content creation, content discovery, and agency-oriented approval workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ContentStudio provides a unified content calendar and scheduler across Facebook, Instagram, X, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, Pinterest, Threads, and Google Business Profile, plus blog publishing to WordPress, Medium, and Shopify.

The platform includes an AI writing assistant and AI image generation, content discovery for trending topics, a unified social inbox, influencer discovery, analytics and reporting, and approval workflows built for agencies managing multiple clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ContentStudio has no permanent free plan, only a free trial. The Standard plan starts around $19/month (1 user, 5 social accounts), the Advanced plan is around $49/month (1 user, 25 accounts), and the Agency Unlimited plan is around $99/month (unlimited users, 50 accounts).

features.description: Native connections to Zapier, Make, n8n, and Pabbly Connect.

Built-in AI writing and image generation reduce reliance on external tools
Approval workflows suit agencies managing multiple clients
Competitive pricing for account-heavy agency use cases
cons: No permanent free plan, only a free trial

Media library storage caps may be tight for high-volume publishers
Company details such as team size are not transparently published

faqs.question: What is ContentStudio used for?
faqs.answer: ContentStudio is used to plan, create, schedule, publish, and analyze content across multiple social media networks and blogs from one dashboard.

faqs.answer: No, ContentStudio offers a free trial rather than a permanent free plan.

faqs.question: What social networks does ContentStudio support?
faqs.answer: ContentStudio supports Facebook, Instagram, X, LinkedIn, TikTok, YouTube, Pinterest, Threads, Google Business Profile, and blog platforms like WordPress and Shopify.

faqs.question: Does ContentStudio include AI content tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes an AI writing assistant for captions and post copy plus AI image generation.

faqs.question: How much does ContentStudio cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start around $19/month for a single user and scale to around $99/month for unlimited users on the Agency plan.

faqs.question: Is ContentStudio good for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Agency Unlimited plan and client approval workflows are designed for agencies managing multiple accounts.

enrichment.short_description: Continuwuity is a free, open-source Rust Matrix homeserver forked from conduwuit for self-hosted, federated chat. See features and deployment options.
enrichment.seo_title: Continuwuity: Open-Source Matrix Homeserver in Rust
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Continuwuity is a free, open-source Rust Matrix homeserver forked from conduwuit. Learn about self-hosting, key features, and how it fits the Matrix ecosystem.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Continuwuity is a free and open-source Matrix protocol homeserver written in Rust, created in April 2025 as a community-driven continuation of conduwuit, a lightweight Rust rewrite of the original Conduit homeserver. Continuwuity was started after conduwuit's original maintainer stepped back and the project was eventually archived, formally archived in January 2026, prompting a group of Matrix enthusiasts, developers, and system administrators, including some former conduwuit contributors alongside new developers, to fork the codebase, keep it actively maintained, fix outstanding bugs, and add features the community wanted rather than let a widely-used lightweight homeserver go unmaintained. The project is released under the Apache License 2.0 and is hosted primarily on the developers' own Forgejo instance and mirrored to GitHub and Codeberg, reflecting the broader Matrix and self-hosting community's preference for federated, non-corporate infrastructure. Continuwuity implements the Matrix specification server-side, meaning it lets an individual or organization run their own chat homeserver that federates with the wider Matrix network, including Element and other Matrix clients and public rooms hosted on matrix.org and elsewhere, rather than depending on a centralized chat provider. Compared to Synapse, the original and most feature-complete Matrix homeserver written in Python, Continuwuity, like conduwuit before it, targets significantly lower resource usage and simpler operations, making it viable to self-host on modest hardware such as a small VPS, a Raspberry Pi, or a home server, which is its primary differentiator and audience: privacy-conscious individuals, small communities, and organizations that want to self-host chat infrastructure without Synapse's heavier PostgreSQL and Python-stack resource footprint. The project ships releases roughly every one to two weeks and documents multiple deployment paths, including Docker, Kubernetes, NixOS packages, and traditional binary installation, with configuration and migration guides for administrators moving from conduwuit or other homeservers, noting that direct database migration from some homeservers, such as the original Conduit or Grapevine, is not supported due to storage-format incompatibilities. Continuwuity also operates or partners on public demo and community Matrix homeservers, such as continuwuity.rocks and federated.nexus, so prospective administrators and end users can try the software or join a hosted instance without deploying their own server first. As a community project rather than a commercial product, Continuwuity has no pricing plans, company headquarters, or employee count; its cost is the server infrastructure and administration time required to self-host it, and support is provided through community channels, such as Matrix chat rooms, and public issue trackers rather than a paid support contract. It competes with and complements other Matrix homeserver implementations, including Synapse, Dendrite, and other Conduit-family forks, as part of the broader decentralized and federated chat ecosystem that also includes protocols like XMPP and ActivityPub-based platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Continuwuity is a free, open-source Matrix homeserver written in Rust, forked in April 2025 as a community continuation of conduwuit after that project's original maintainer stepped back and it was eventually archived. It is maintained by a group of Matrix enthusiasts, developers, and system administrators, including former conduwuit contributors.

The project implements the Matrix protocol server-side under the Apache License 2.0, allowing anyone to self-host a chat server that federates with the wider Matrix network, including Element and other Matrix clients.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Continuwuity is designed to be lightweight and efficient, running on modest hardware such as a small VPS or Raspberry Pi, in contrast to the heavier resource footprint of Synapse, the original Matrix homeserver. It supports appservice bridges for connecting to external platforms and ships new releases roughly every one to two weeks.

Deployment options include Docker, Kubernetes, NixOS packages, and traditional binary installation, with documentation covering migration from conduwuit installs. Public demo homeservers such as continuwuity.rocks let people try the software before self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Continuwuity is free, open-source software with no licensing cost. As a self-hosted project, the only expenses are server infrastructure and administration time; support comes from community Matrix channels and public issue trackers rather than a paid contract.

features.title: Matrix Protocol Federation
features.description: Full server-side implementation of the Matrix specification for federated, decentralized chat.

features.title: Rust-based Low Resource Usage
features.description: Built in Rust to run efficiently on modest hardware compared to Python-based homeservers like Synapse.

features.title: Appservice Bridge Support
features.description: Supports bridging to external chat platforms via Matrix appservices.

features.title: Multiple Deployment Methods
features.description: Docker, Kubernetes, NixOS, and traditional binary installation are all documented.

features.title: Frequent Releases
features.description: New releases are cut roughly every one to two weeks.

features.title: Migration Tooling
features.description: Documentation and tooling for migrating from existing conduwuit installations.

features.title: Public Demo Homeservers
features.description: Community-run instances like continuwuity.rocks let users try Matrix without self-hosting first.

pros: Free, open-source, and self-hosted with no licensing cost
Lightweight enough to run on modest hardware
Actively maintained with frequent releases, unlike the archived conduwuit
Full interoperability with the wider Matrix and Element ecosystem
cons: Requires self-hosting expertise in server administration and the Rust ecosystem
Young project, forked in 2025, with a shorter track record than Synapse
No official commercial support or SLA
Migration from some other homeservers, such as the original Conduit or Grapevine, is not supported

faqs.question: What is Continuwuity?
faqs.answer: Continuwuity is a free, open-source Matrix homeserver written in Rust, forked from conduwuit as a community-maintained continuation of the project.

faqs.question: Is Continuwuity free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Continuwuity is free and open source under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: What language is Continuwuity written in?
faqs.answer: Continuwuity is written primarily in Rust.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Continuwuity?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting is the primary way to run Continuwuity, with Docker, Kubernetes, NixOS, and binary installation options documented.

faqs.question: Is Continuwuity compatible with Matrix clients like Element?
faqs.answer: Yes, Continuwuity implements the Matrix protocol and federates with the wider Matrix network, including Element clients.

faqs.question: Why was Continuwuity created?
faqs.answer: Continuwuity was created after the original conduwuit project's maintainer stepped back and the project was archived, so community contributors forked it to keep it maintained.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted Team or Community Chat
use_cases.description: Organizations and communities run their own federated chat server instead of relying on a centralized provider.

use_cases.title: Lightweight Homelab Matrix Server
use_cases.description: Self-hosters run Continuwuity on a small VPS or Raspberry Pi as part of a personal homelab setup.

enrichment.short_description: Contractbook is a Copenhagen-based contract lifecycle management platform for drafting, e-signing, storing, and automating contracts, now part of Scrive.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Contractbook offers contract drafting, e-signing, and lifecycle automation for growing businesses. See key features, pricing tiers, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Contractbook is a contract lifecycle management platform founded in 2017 in Copenhagen, Denmark, where the company remains headquartered. It raised venture funding from investors including Tiger Global, Bessemer Venture Partners, and Gradient Ventures, and reports serving more than 250,000 users across more than 85 countries, primarily small and mid-sized businesses that want to move contract creation, signing, storage, and management off email and paper without adopting a heavyweight enterprise CLM suite. In June 2025, Contractbook was acquired by Scrive, a Nordic e-signature and digital contract company, and now operates as part of the Scrive group, combining Contractbook's contract-lifecycle and automation tooling with Scrive's e-signature and identity-verification infrastructure, including support for Nordic digital ID schemes such as BankID and NemID/MitID. The platform's core workflow covers the full contract lifecycle: drafting contracts from a library of customizable templates, collaborating on and redlining drafts with counterparties, collecting legally binding electronic signatures, storing signed and unsigned contracts in a searchable repository, and tracking key dates, obligations, and renewal deadlines so teams do not miss contract expirations. Contractbook also offers AI-assisted features, including an AI contract assistant for drafting help and AI-powered data extraction that can pull key terms and metadata out of uploaded, non-native contract documents, plus CSV export and reporting for compliance and audit purposes. Pricing is structured as tiered, named subscription plans rather than simple per-seat pricing: entry-level plans are aimed at solo operators or very small teams with a limited number of users, templates, and signed contracts per year, while mid- and upper-tier plans aimed at growing and scaling companies raise the user count, template library size, and annual signed-contract volume, and add automation, dynamic templates, user provisioning, a dedicated Customer Success Manager, and white-glove onboarding. A custom or enterprise tier is available for organizations that need deeper technical customization, API integrations, and dedicated implementation consultancy; Contractbook's marketing states customers can save up to 25% by choosing annual billing over monthly billing, and a 14-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required is offered for prospective customers to evaluate the platform. Because pricing is presented as bundled named tiers rather than transparent flat per-user rates and has changed following the Scrive acquisition, prospective buyers should confirm current figures directly with Contractbook or Scrive sales rather than relying on older third-party pricing roundups. Contractbook competes with other contract lifecycle and e-signature platforms such as PandaDoc, DocuSign CLM, Ironclad, and Concord, differentiating primarily on its all-in-one drafting-to-signature-to-repository workflow, its strong footprint in the Nordic and broader European market, and its now-combined offering with Scrive's e-signature and identity-verification technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Contractbook is a contract lifecycle management platform founded in 2017 in Copenhagen, Denmark. It has raised venture funding from investors including Tiger Global and Bessemer Venture Partners and reports serving more than 250,000 users across 85+ countries.

In June 2025, Contractbook was acquired by Scrive, a Nordic e-signature company, and now operates as part of the Scrive group, pairing Contractbook's contract-lifecycle tooling with Scrive's e-signature and digital identity infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Contractbook covers the full contract lifecycle: drafting from customizable templates, collaborative redlining, legally binding e-signatures (including Nordic digital ID schemes like BankID and NemID/MitID), a searchable contract repository, and obligation and renewal-date tracking.

AI-assisted features include a contract drafting assistant and AI-powered data extraction that pulls key terms and metadata from uploaded contract documents, alongside CSV export and reporting for compliance and audit needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Contractbook uses named, tiered subscription plans (Essential, Centralize, Accelerate, and a custom Enterprise tier) that scale by user count, template library size, and annual signed-contract volume rather than simple per-seat pricing. A 14-day free trial is available, and annual billing can save up to 25% versus monthly billing. Exact current prices should be confirmed with sales, since figures vary across sources and pricing has evolved since the 2025 Scrive acquisition.

features.description: Store and search signed and unsigned contracts in one place.

features.title: Obligation & Renewal Tracking
features.description: Track key dates and obligations so teams do not miss contract renewals or expirations.

features.title: Approval & Automation Workflows
features.description: Automations and dynamic templates streamline recurring contract processes on higher tiers.

features.title: Reporting & CSV Export
features.description: Export contract data and generate reports for compliance and audit purposes.

pros: Covers the full contract lifecycle in one platform
Strong Nordic and European e-signature and digital ID support
AI-assisted drafting and data extraction speed up contract creation
Now backed by Scrive's signature and identity-verification infrastructure
cons: Named pricing tiers make direct cost comparison harder than flat per-seat pricing
Entry tiers cap users, templates, and annual contract volume
Publicly reported pricing details vary across sources and may be outdated post-acquisition
Deeper integrations and customization require the custom/enterprise tier

faqs.question: What is Contractbook?
faqs.answer: Contractbook is a contract lifecycle management platform for drafting, e-signing, storing, and automating contracts.

faqs.question: Is Contractbook free?
faqs.answer: Contractbook offers a 14-day free trial but does not have a permanent free plan.

faqs.question: Who owns Contractbook?
faqs.answer: Contractbook has been part of Scrive, a Nordic e-signature company, since it was acquired in June 2025.

faqs.question: Does Contractbook support e-signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, including support for Nordic digital identity schemes such as BankID and NemID/MitID.

faqs.question: Where is Contractbook based?
faqs.answer: Contractbook is headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark.

faqs.question: Does Contractbook offer an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, API integrations are available, particularly on the higher and custom pricing tiers.

enrichment.short_description: ContractWorks, an Onit product, is a contract management platform offering AI-driven extraction, secure storage, and e-signatures at a flat monthly rate for unlimited users.
enrichment.headquarters: Houston, Texas, US (now part of Onit, headquartered in Austin, Texas)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ContractWorks, an Onit product, offers AI-powered contract management with flat-rate pricing for unlimited users. See features, plans, pros, and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ContractWorks is a contract lifecycle management platform founded in 2013 and originally headquartered in Houston, Texas. The company was acquired by Onit, a legal operations and enterprise legal management software provider headquartered in Austin, Texas, and ContractWorks now operates as an Onit product, with the two brands increasingly unified under Onit's platform and marketing; customer-facing materials now typically refer to it as "ContractWorks, an Onit product." ContractWorks is positioned as a scalable, easier-to-adopt alternative to complex enterprise CLM suites, aimed at legal, finance, procurement, and operations teams that need centralized contract storage, tracking, and reporting without a long implementation cycle. Its core capabilities include secure, encrypted cloud storage for contracts and related documents; AI-driven contract data extraction and auto-tagging that automatically identifies and tags key fields such as expiration dates, obligations, parties, and governing terms upon upload, reducing manual data entry; built-in electronic signatures for drafting and executing agreements without a separate e-signature tool; customizable reporting and dashboards for tracking contract status, renewal dates, and compliance obligations; granular user permissions and audit trails; and expanded API and integration capabilities following the Onit acquisition, allowing ContractWorks to connect with other systems in a broader legal-ops or procurement tech stack. A distinguishing feature of ContractWorks' commercial model is flat-rate, unlimited-user pricing: rather than charging per seat, ContractWorks publishes a starting price of around $600 per month that includes unlimited users, a dedicated account manager, and no separate implementation, training, or data-migration fees, a structure aimed at organizations that want many employees, not just a small legal team, to have visibility into contracts without per-seat cost scaling. Exact plan tiers above the entry price, such as for higher storage, more advanced AI extraction, or additional integrations, are quoted directly by ContractWorks and Onit sales rather than published in full detail. ContractWorks competes with other mid-market and enterprise CLM tools such as Ironclad, Concord, PandaDoc, and ContractPodAi, as well as its own corporate sibling products within Onit's broader legal-ops suite, and is most often selected by legal and compliance teams at mid-sized companies that want AI-assisted organization of an existing contract backlog plus straightforward e-signature and reporting, without adopting a full enterprise-grade CLM implementation project. The product is delivered as a cloud-based SaaS web application; there is no self-hosted or open-source deployment option, and access is provided through a responsive web interface rather than dedicated native mobile apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ContractWorks is a contract lifecycle management platform founded in 2013 in Houston, Texas. It was later acquired by Onit, a legal operations software company headquartered in Austin, Texas, and now operates as "ContractWorks, an Onit product," unified increasingly under the Onit brand.

The platform is positioned as an easier-to-adopt alternative to complex enterprise CLM suites, aimed at legal, finance, and procurement teams that want centralized contract storage and tracking without a long implementation project.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include secure encrypted document storage, AI-driven contract data extraction and auto-tagging of key fields like expiration dates and obligations, built-in electronic signatures, customizable reporting dashboards, and granular user permissions with audit trails.
Following the Onit acquisition, ContractWorks has expanded its API and integration capabilities, allowing it to connect with other systems in a broader legal-operations or procurement tech stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ContractWorks uses flat-rate, unlimited-user pricing rather than per-seat billing, starting at around $600 per month, which includes a dedicated account manager and no separate implementation, training, or migration fees. Pricing above the entry tier is quoted directly by the ContractWorks/Onit sales team.

features.title: AI-driven Data Extraction & Auto-tagging

features.title: Secure Encrypted Storage
features.description: Centralized, encrypted cloud storage for contracts and related documents.

features.description: Draft and execute agreements without needing a separate e-signature tool.

features.title: Customizable Reporting & Dashboards
features.description: Track contract status, renewal dates, and compliance obligations.

features.title: Renewal & Obligation Alerts
features.description: Automated tracking of key dates so teams do not miss renewals or deadlines.

features.title: Granular Permissions & Audit Trails
features.description: Control access at a granular level and maintain a full audit history.

features.title: Flat-rate Unlimited-user Pricing
features.description: One flat monthly price covers unlimited users rather than per-seat billing.

features.title: Expanded API & Integrations
features.description: Broader integration capabilities following the Onit acquisition.

pros: Flat-rate pricing removes per-seat cost scaling for large teams
AI auto-tagging speeds up organizing an existing contract backlog
Built-in e-signatures avoid needing a separate tool
No extra fees for implementation, training, or migration
cons: Entry price of around $600/month is a meaningful commitment for very small teams
Detailed pricing above the entry tier is not fully public
Less configurable than dedicated enterprise CLM platforms for complex workflows
No dedicated native mobile apps; access is web-only

faqs.question: What is ContractWorks?
faqs.answer: ContractWorks is a contract lifecycle management platform, now sold as an Onit product, offering AI-driven extraction, secure storage, and e-signatures.

faqs.question: How much does ContractWorks cost?
faqs.answer: ContractWorks starts at around $600 per month with unlimited users included.

faqs.question: Does ContractWorks include e-signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, built-in electronic signatures are included without needing a separate tool.

faqs.question: Is ContractWorks the same as Onit?
faqs.answer: ContractWorks is a product owned and marketed by Onit, a legal operations software company; the two are increasingly unified under the Onit brand.

faqs.question: Does ContractWorks use AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, it uses AI to extract and auto-tag key contract data such as dates and obligations on upload.

faqs.question: Who founded ContractWorks?
faqs.answer: ContractWorks was founded in 2013 in Houston, Texas, and was later acquired by Onit.
